blocks.title: Overview
blocks.text:
Integration Arc: Sessions 12–16. The first eight sessions of this course built the inner equipment of the ministry leader — self-awareness, values clarity, and the relational posture of an outward mindset. Sessions 9–11 turned that formation outward into community: reading context, building trust, and engaging neighbors as assets. Sessions 12–16 bring both movements together. Before moving into the content of this session, name what you are carrying into it. From Sessions 2–3 you have a working knowledge of your own values and the way those values can quietly steer decisions before you are conscious of choosing. From the outward mindset work you have a practiced orientation toward others as people with capacities and constraints rather than objects to be managed. From Sessions 9–11 you have tools for reading a community's assets, its relational fabric, and the forces shaping its future. This closing arc does not introduce a separate set of skills. It asks what happens when all of those capabilities operate at the same moment, under the same pressure, on the same decision. That is where this session begins.

This closing arc is where personal formation and community engagement become coordinated strategy — where a leader stops developing and deploying skills separately and begins using them together, under pressure, in an uncertain world.

Ministry leaders are not typically trained to plan for uncertainty — they are trained to plan for goals. But the rate of change in urban communities, funding landscapes, demographic shifts, and cultural dynamics makes goal-based planning alone dangerously fragile. What happens when the one large grant your ministry depends on disappears? When a gentrification wave displaces the community you have been serving? When the next generation of young leaders does not share the ministry's founding vision? Scenario Planning is the planning discipline developed precisely for these conditions — and this session introduces it as both an intellectual framework and a practical ministry tool.

Notice how quickly the values-clarification work from Sessions 2–3 becomes relevant here. A leader who has not done that interior work will approach scenario planning as a purely analytical exercise — a forecasting technique borrowed from the corporate world. A leader who has done that work brings something different to the table: a settled sense of what the ministry must remain true to regardless of which future arrives. Values clarity is not window dressing for strategic planning. It is the anchor that keeps scenario thinking from drifting into either paralysis or cynicism. Without it, a leader who looks at four plausible futures may simply feel overwhelmed. With it, the leader has a reference point that survives all four.

The Fast Forward Planning™ process (developed by Peter O'Donnell and used in the DVULI curriculum) provides an efficient five-step framework: (1) Ask a Focusing Question — the critical concern about the ministry's future; (2) Identify Driving Forces — the external and internal factors that will shape that future; (3) Surface Questions That Matter — the critical uncertainties that you don't know the answers to but must prepare for; (4) Explore Alternative Scenarios — two or more plausible, distinctly different stories about how the future might unfold; and (5) Commit to Strategic Priorities — the high-leverage actions that prepare the ministry for multiple possible futures rather than betting everything on one. The Sandra Cestero and Randall Jones case studies from the DVULI curriculum illustrate each step of the process in a youth ministry context that participants recognize from Session 11.

The Driving Forces step is worth pausing on. When a participant asks what forces are shaping their ministry's future, they are doing community-reading work — the same work practiced in Sessions 9–11. Asset mapping taught you to look at what a neighborhood holds. Context reading taught you to interpret what forces are moving through it. Driving Forces analysis is that skill applied directly to strategic planning. The community intelligence you built is not background material. It is primary data for this exercise.

The philosophical anchor of this session is the Luke 14:28–30 passage about counting the cost before building a tower — not as a cautionary tale about timidity but as a wisdom invitation to plan with both faith and intelligence. The session's most powerful tool is the Big Grid — the two-by-two matrix of Stagnation vs. Innovation and Isolation vs. Collaboration that generates four scenario archetypes: Monument (doing the same things, alone), Lifeboat (drifting together in community), Pioneer (innovating alone), and Movement (innovating in collaborative community). The Movement quadrant is the Breakthrough scenario — the one toward which all five Core Values, all the Community Youth Development work, and all the resource networking of subsequent sessions are oriented.

The outward mindset introduced earlier in the course is visible in the structure of the grid itself. The horizontal axis — Isolation vs. Collaboration — is not a neutral planning variable. It is a direct expression of the relational posture that outward mindset work was building. A leader who has genuinely internalized that posture will feel the difference between the Pioneer and Movement quadrants not as a conceptual distinction but as a recognition. Pioneer sounds capable. Movement sounds like what the work is actually for.

Convergence: A participant using this session well will bring their values inventory, their community asset map, and their outward mindset commitments into the same room as the Big Grid and the Focusing Question. They will notice that the scenario they fear most — Ministry as Monument — is not primarily a resource problem or a demographic problem. It is a formation problem, one that the interior work of Sessions 1–8 was designed to prevent. And they will notice that the scenario they are aiming for — Ministry as Movement — requires precisely the combination of self-knowledge, community trust, and collaborative posture that the entire course has been building. The ministry decision in front of them, whatever it is, looks different when they can see which quadrant it is pointing toward and which prior commitments it is either honoring or quietly abandoning.

Session 12

Remembering the Future

Scenario Planning as a Tool for Ministry Leadership in an Uncertain and Rapidly Changing World

It was a Tuesday afternoon when Randall Jones received the call he had been dreading. The foundation that had provided forty percent of his ministry's operating budget for the past three years was redirecting its grant portfolio. His funding would not be renewed. In the same month, a long-simmering gentrification wave in the neighborhood surrounding his ministry had accelerated — four blocks of affordable housing were sold to a developer, and more than sixty of his most active youth participants were displaced to communities across the city. A few weeks later, two of his most gifted young leaders told him they were heading to seminary and would not be returning. In fewer than ninety days, Randall's well-run, faithfully-led ministry had been destabilized by forces that were, each in their own right, predictable. The trend data was visible to anyone paying attention. The foundation had signaled its pivot in its annual report. The gentrification pressure had been reported in The Jackson Times. The seminary call on young leaders was a decades-old pattern. Randall had simply planned for one future — his current one — when the world around him was quietly producing several others.

This session is about what Randall needed before that Tuesday call ever arrived: a planning discipline built not for certainty, but for honest, faithful engagement with uncertainty. Scenario Planning does not promise to predict the future. It promises something more useful — the capacity to prepare for multiple futures simultaneously, so that when the world changes, your ministry does not collapse. It changes with wisdom, with faith, and with a strategy that was built for exactly this moment.

The Planning Problem in Ministry

Ministry leaders are not typically trained to plan for uncertainty. They are trained to plan for goals. Strategic plans identify vision and milestones. Operational plans translate those milestones into tasks. Personal schedules align daily action with those tasks. This approach to planning — what we might call goal-based planning — is deeply embedded in organizational culture, and for good reason: when circumstances are stable and largely predictable, goal-based planning works. It drives focus, alignment, and accountability.

But the rate of change in urban communities, funding landscapes, demographic shifts, and cultural dynamics makes goal-based planning alone dangerously fragile in the environments where most DVULI participants serve (Allison & Kaye, 2015). The youth organizations studied by Villarruel and colleagues demonstrate that youth-serving organizations are especially vulnerable to three categories of environmental disruption: funding volatility driven by shifting philanthropic priorities, demographic change driven by migration and gentrification, and leadership pipeline disruption as gifted young leaders pursue multiple competing callings (Villarruel et al., 2003). Each of these categories is not an emergency — it is a system. Each one develops slowly, announces itself through early indicators, and arrives as a crisis only for those who were not watching.

Walker and Salt (2006) describe this vulnerability in ecological terms: systems that are over-optimized for one predicted future become brittle when conditions change. An organization that bets its entire resource structure, staffing model, and ministry strategy on a single scenario — a single story about how the future will unfold — has reduced its adaptive capacity to near zero. What resilience thinking argues for, and what scenario planning delivers, is the organizational equivalent of biodiversity: a portfolio of strategic capacities that can function across a range of plausible futures, not just the most comfortable one.

Think About It

Before reading further, pause to recall the last major disruption your ministry experienced that came as a surprise. Looking back, were there early signals that this change was coming? What would you have needed to do differently in order to have been prepared? Keep this experience in mind as you work through the Fast Forward Planning™ framework in this session.

What Is Scenario Planning?

Scenario planning is a disciplined method for imagining possible futures and developing robust strategies that serve organizational health across a range of those futures (Schoemaker, 1995). It was developed in its modern form by the Royal Dutch Shell company in the late 1960s as a response to the volatility of global energy markets, and was later refined by practitioners like Peter Schwartz and Kees van der Heijden into a widely applicable methodology for strategic leadership (Schwartz, 1991; van der Heijden, 1996).

At its philosophical core, scenario planning rests on three premises. First, no one can predict the future — and the faster the pace of change, the less reliable any single prediction becomes. Second, despite uncertainty, leaders still must make decisions, allocate resources, and take action. A paralyzed organization is not a faithful one. Third, it is possible to develop strategies that are robust — that serve the organization well across multiple different futures rather than being optimized for just one (Ringland, Sparrow, & Lustig, 2010). The goal of scenario planning, therefore, is not a more accurate picture of tomorrow. It is better decisions about the future.

"The test of a good scenario is not whether it portrays the future accurately, but whether it enables an organization to learn and adapt."

— Peter Schwartz, The Art of the Long View (1991)

Scenarios are not forecasts, and they are not best-case or worst-case projections. They are plausible, distinctly different stories about how the future might unfold, written from the vantage point of looking back — as if the future had already happened. This technique, which the DVULI curriculum calls "remembering the future," forces strategic leaders to move beyond abstract analysis and inhabit the texture of a possible reality in specific, concrete, and narrative terms. According to Chermack (2011), the quality of scenario stories is a better predictor of strategic planning effectiveness than the sophistication of the analytical models that surround them — the stories must be vivid enough to be believable, different enough to force genuine strategic choice, and written with enough specificity that they reveal concrete action priorities.

The Fast Forward Planning™ Framework

The DVULI curriculum employs the Fast Forward Planning™ process, developed by Peter O'Donnell, as the primary vehicle for introducing scenario planning to ministry leaders. Its five-step structure is efficient, accessible, and specifically designed for community-based nonprofit organizations and faith-driven ministries — organizations that often lack dedicated planning staff but face planning challenges just as complex as those confronting corporate enterprises. The five steps move sequentially from a sharp focusing question through the identification of driving forces, the surfacing of critical uncertainties, the construction of alternative scenarios, and finally the identification of strategic priorities that prepare the ministry for multiple possible futures.

Step 1: Ask a Focusing Question

Every scenario planning process begins with a question — a carefully constructed inquiry that defines the scope of the planning effort and orients all subsequent analysis. Chermack (2011) identifies the focusing question as the single most critical element in the entire scenario planning process, noting that poorly formed focusing questions are the most common failure point in scenario planning practice. A focusing question that is too operational ("Should we hire a second youth pastor?") directs attention to a single decision rather than to the conditions that make that decision meaningful. A focusing question that is too abstract ("What is the future of urban ministry?") fails to connect to the specific context, constraints, and opportunities of the particular organization doing the planning.

The ideal focusing question addresses a strategic concern about the future — typically a concern about the organization's fundamental viability, direction, or impact over a two-to-five year horizon. When Sandra Cestero, the youth leader at Grace Christian Family Center, applied Fast Forward Planning™ to her situation, her focusing question was: "What will the youth ministry of this church look like in two to three years?" That question is neither so narrow that it forecloses strategic thinking nor so broad that it fails to anchor the scenarios in her actual ministry context. It points toward the deep concerns she had been praying about — the aging congregation, the growing community need, the unclear relationship between church-based and community-based ministry — and opens the space for genuine strategic exploration.

Step 2: Identify Driving Forces

With a focusing question established, the planner then identifies the driving forces — the external and internal factors that will shape the answer to that question. Schwartz (1991) distinguishes between predetermined elements (factors whose direction is already fixed and predictable, like demographic trends or known funding commitments) and critical uncertainties (factors whose direction is genuinely unknown and could unfold in multiple different ways). Van der Heijden (1996) refines this further, arguing that the most strategically important driving forces are those that are both high impact (they will significantly affect the focal question no matter what) and high uncertainty (the planner genuinely cannot know how they will unfold). It is this combination — high impact plus high uncertainty — that demands scenario preparation rather than simple forecasting or goal-setting.

In Sandra's case, driving forces included the number of church youth and their needs, the nature and severity of youth-related community problems, the church's openness to community ministry, the availability of volunteers, and her own capacity to balance competing demands. Some of these forces were relatively predictable: the aging congregation was a demographic trend with clear trajectory. Others were genuinely uncertain: the community's receptiveness to church-initiated programs, and the extent to which church leadership would support or resist a strategic pivot.

Step 3: Surface Questions That Matter

The third step transforms driving forces into questions. The most useful questions are those whose answers the planner does not already know — questions that represent genuine forks in the road of possible futures. If Sandra already knew whether her church would support community ministry, she would not need a scenario; she would simply act on that knowledge. It is precisely because she does not know — and because the answer will profoundly shape every subsequent strategic choice — that the question needs to be surfaced, named, and held. This step is what one workshop participant memorably described as "becoming more certain about your uncertainties."

Questions That Matter are not questions about tactics. They are not "How many volunteers do we need?" They are questions about the conditions under which those tactics will either work or fail: "Will our church community understand and support an expanded role in neighborhood ministry, and if so, how quickly?" That is a question whose answer will determine whether the next three years unfold as empowering breakthrough or as exhausting resistance.

Step 4: Explore Alternative Scenarios

With a set of critical uncertainties named, the planner now constructs at least two — and ideally three or four — alternative scenarios: plausible, distinctly different stories about how the future might unfold. The "remembering the future" technique instructs the planner to write these stories in the past tense, from a vantage point two or three years from now, describing what happened as if looking back. This narrative orientation matters enormously. Strategic plans tend to be written as aspirational maps: "We will do X, then Y, then Z." Scenario stories are written as accounts of a lived past: "We found that… The community responded by… What we had not anticipated was…" This shift in temporal perspective activates different cognitive faculties — more intuitive, more sensitive to second-order effects, more honest about the ways in which the best-laid plans encounter resistance in the real world.

The scenarios Sandra developed ranged from a story of surprising community partnership, empowered volunteers, and deep community transformation to a story of institutional resistance, isolation, and eventually resignation from ministry. Neither story was a caricature. Both were grounded in the same driving forces and the same set of uncertainties — they differed in which way those uncertainties resolved. Both were plausible. Both were written with enough specificity that they revealed concrete implications for strategic action. And both, taken together, gave Sandra a richer and more honest picture of the strategic terrain ahead of her than any single goal-based plan could have provided.

Step 5: Commit to Strategic Priorities

The final step translates scenario insight into action. The DVULI curriculum identifies four types of strategic priorities:

  • Just Do It — high-leverage actions the leader should take regardless of which scenario unfolds, for which the necessary capacity already exists
  • Develop Essential Capacities — actions that should be taken regardless of scenario but require capacity building before they can be implemented effectively
  • Prepare Contingency Plans — actions that depend on how specific uncertainties resolve, requiring advance planning so the ministry can act quickly when conditions clarify
  • Access Additional Capacities — resources and capabilities the ministry does not currently possess but may need depending on how futures unfold

For Sandra, this final step revealed that all five of her highest-priority actions were capacity-building activities — establishing an accountability group, mapping community assets, building church support, building community relationships, and developing volunteer leaders. None of these were contingent on knowing the future. All of them were robust: they would improve her ministry's strategic position across every scenario she had imagined. This is the hallmark of a good scenario planning outcome — not a single correct answer, but a set of high-leverage investments in organizational resilience that serve the ministry well regardless of which future arrives.

The Sandra Cestero and Randall Jones Case Studies in Practice

The Jackson city group participants — Sandra Cestero, Randall Jones, Danny Agosto, Leroy Burnett, and their colleagues — provide a running case study for the application of Fast Forward Planning™ throughout the DVULI curriculum. Their contexts are recognizably urban, their challenges are authentically ministry-specific, and their scenarios illuminate the five-step process in ways that are immediately transferable to participants' own settings.

Randall Jones's situation — described in both the Local Workshop 2 workbook and the Systems Thinking case study from National Conference 2 — is particularly instructive for this session. When a large national urban youth organization announces plans to move into his neighborhood, Randall faces a textbook scenario planning moment. His current ministry model was built for a particular competitive and collaborative landscape. The arrival of a well-resourced national organization could unfold in any of several distinctly different ways: as a threat that displaces his programming and his volunteers; as a partnership opportunity that dramatically extends his reach; or as a neutral coexistence in which both organizations occupy different segments of the community's youth development ecosystem. Each of these outcomes is plausible. Each demands a different strategic posture. And none of them can be predicted with confidence in advance of the organization's actual arrival.

Applying Fast Forward Planning™ to Randall's situation begins with a focusing question: "What will the role of my ministry in youth recreation outreach look like in two to three years, given the changing organizational landscape?" From that question, driving forces emerge: the national organization's actual programming scope, Randall's current depth of community relationships and trust, the availability of local funding that might be competed for or shared, and the cultural fit between the new organization's approach and the community's established expectations. Questions That Matter include: "Will the national organization seek to partner with existing ministries or establish a competing presence?" and "Do my current community relationships provide enough trust capital to retain participant loyalty under competitive pressure?" Two to four scenarios — from deep collaborative partnership to aggressive competition to strategic differentiation — would then reveal a set of strategic priorities that Randall can begin building now, before the situation resolves.

Think About It

Consider your own ministry's three most significant sources of funding, your three most active volunteer leaders, and the three neighborhoods or communities you most depend on for participants. What would your ministry look like in two years if any two of these changed dramatically and simultaneously? Have you planned for that possibility? What would your focusing question be for a scenario planning exercise centered on your ministry's most critical vulnerabilities?

Widget 1: Fast Forward Planning™ Interactive Flowchart

Work through all five steps of the Fast Forward Planning™ process for a specific concern in your own ministry. At each step, follow the guided prompts to develop your own scenario plan.

⚙️ Fast Forward Planning™ — Five-Step Process
Step 1: Ask a Focusing Question
A strong focusing question is strategic (not operational), forward-looking (2–3 years), and tied to your ministry's core concerns. Avoid questions that are too narrow ("Should I hire staff?") or too vague ("What is the future?"). Aim for something like: "What will [aspect of my ministry] look like in two to three years, given [key challenge or opportunity]?"
Step 2: Identify Driving Forces
List up to 8 factors (external or internal) that will shape your focusing question's answer. Rate each on Impact (1–5: how much will it affect your ministry?) and Uncertainty (1–5: how much do you NOT know how it will unfold?). Combined scores above 15 are Critical Uncertainties — these demand scenario preparation.
Step 3: Surface Questions That Matter
Your highest-scoring (Critical Uncertainty) driving forces automatically generate question stems below. Complete each question to articulate the genuine strategic uncertainty that drives it. These are questions whose answers you don't know but must be prepared for.
  • Your critical uncertainties from Step 2 will appear here as question stems to complete.
Step 4: Write Alternative Scenarios
Write two distinctly different stories about your ministry's future, told in the past tense as if you are looking back 2–3 years from now. Each story should reflect a different answer to your key uncertainties. Focus on outcomes and what actually happened — not on plans or intentions. Aim for at least 3–4 sentences per scenario.
Step 5: Commit to Strategic Priorities
Based on your scenarios, identify specific strategic priorities and drag or type them into the appropriate category below. Just Do It priorities are high-leverage and ready to act on now. Develop Essential Capacities actions need capacity building first. Contingency Plans depend on which scenario unfolds. Access Additional Capacities are resources you may need to find.
✅ Just Do It
High-leverage. Do now. Capacity exists.
🔧 Develop Essential Capacities
Must do, but build capacity first.
📋 Prepare Contingency Plans
Depends on which future arrives.
🔍 Access Additional Capacities
Resources to identify and secure.

The Biblical Wisdom of Counting the Cost

The philosophical anchor of this session is Luke 14:28–30, in which Jesus asks: "Suppose one of you wants to build a tower. Won't you first sit down and estimate the cost to see if you have enough money to complete it? For if you lay the foundation and are not able to finish it, everyone who sees it will ridicule you, saying, 'This person began to build and wasn't able to finish.'" (NIV)

This passage is often read as a cautionary tale about the hazards of ambition — a warning to be humble about ministry aspirations. But in context, Jesus is offering a wisdom invitation: the kind of planning that honors the seriousness of a calling, that respects the complexity of the work, and that refuses the false piety of action without discernment. The builder who does not count the cost does not display great faith — the builder displays poor stewardship. Scenario planning is, in this light, an act of discipleship: the disciplined, prayerful, honest engagement with the conditions under which God has called us to build.

This integration of faith and intelligence is a recurring theme in the DVULI Core Values framework. The Core Value of Leverage — perhaps the most directly relevant to scenario planning — calls leaders to look for ways in which seemingly small actions produce disproportionately large results. But Leverage is only possible when the leader has done the prior work of understanding the system: knowing which driving forces are at work, which uncertainties could resolve in ways that amplify or negate any given action, and which strategic investments will retain their value across a range of possible futures. Scenario planning is the intelligence work that makes Leverage possible.

The Big Grid: Four Ministry Future Scenarios

The DVULI curriculum's most powerful scenario planning tool for ministry leaders is the Big Grid — a two-by-two matrix that maps four archetypal ministry futures along two axes: Stagnation versus Innovation, and Isolation versus Collaboration. The intersection of these two dimensions generates four named scenarios, each of which carries a distinct set of strategic implications for the ministry that finds itself living inside it.

Monument: Stagnation + Isolation

The Monument scenario describes a ministry that continues to do the same things, in the same ways, alone, while the world around it changes. Monument ministries are often faithful in their commitment to their original calling, but their faithfulness has calcified into routine. They have not developed new strategies, built new relationships, or adapted their methods to the communities they now serve — communities that may look quite different from the ones the ministry was founded to reach. Monument organizations frequently cite their history as evidence of their value, but history, unconnected to present relevance, becomes a memorial rather than a living mission. This is the scenario toward which many long-established urban ministries drift without active intervention.

Lifeboat: Stagnation + Collaboration

The Lifeboat scenario describes a ministry that has successfully built collaborative relationships but has not developed an innovative strategy. Lifeboat organizations are not isolated — they know their partners, they attend the right coalitions, they participate in community conversations. But they drift together rather than rowing purposefully. Collaboration without strategic direction produces a form of mutual comfort that can be mistaken for progress. The relationships are real and valuable, but they are not generating new approaches to persistent problems. Lifeboat organizations often cite their networks as evidence of health, but networks without shared innovation are social infrastructure without transformational impact.

Pioneer: Innovation + Isolation

The Pioneer scenario describes a ministry that has developed new, creative, and effective approaches to its work, but that is doing so alone. Pioneer organizations are often led by visionary, high-energy leaders who are so committed to their breakthrough ideas that they have neither the time nor the inclination to build the collaborative relationships that would amplify their impact. Pioneer ministries can achieve remarkable results in the short term, but they are fragile: dependent on a single leader's energy, cut off from the resource networks that would sustain and scale their innovation, and frequently unknown to the broader community of practice that could learn from them. Burnout is a near-universal outcome in the Pioneer quadrant.

Movement: Innovation + Collaboration

The Movement scenario is the Breakthrough scenario — the quadrant toward which all five DVULI Core Values, all Community Youth Development practice, and all resource networking strategy are oriented. A Movement ministry is both innovating and collaborating: it is developing new, contextually responsive approaches to youth ministry and community development, and it is doing so in deep partnership with a growing network of aligned organizations, community members, and resource partners. Movement organizations are greater than the sum of their parts. Their innovations are multiplied by their networks. Their networks are energized by their innovations. And the community impact they generate is both deeper and more durable than anything achievable in isolation or through stagnation.

Ringland, Sparrow, and Lustig (2010) observe that the most valuable outcome of a scenario planning process is not the scenarios themselves but the strategic conversation they generate — a reframing of assumptions and a shared vocabulary for discussing the future. The Big Grid is precisely this kind of strategic conversation tool. By giving names to four distinct organizational futures, it creates a shared language through which ministry teams can honestly assess their current trajectory and commit to the strategic investments that would move them toward Movement. Schoemaker (1995) notes that the power of a two-by-two scenario matrix lies in its capacity to hold multiple futures in tension simultaneously — not collapsing them into a single prediction, but keeping them all present to the strategic imagination.

Strategic Priority Recommendations

    Scenario Planning and the DVULI Core Values

    Scenario planning is not an isolated technique — it is the strategic expression of all five DVULI Core Values applied simultaneously to the future of a ministry. Accountability demands honest assessment of current reality, including honest acknowledgment of the uncertainties that current plans have papered over. Balance calls the leader to build into the ministry's strategy the kind of adaptive capacity that protects against the burnout and organizational fragility that single-scenario planning produces. Interdependence is the relational infrastructure that makes the Movement scenario possible — and the scenario planning process itself, when done well, is a practice in collaborative intelligence, bringing multiple perspectives to bear on an uncertain future. Empowerment asks what kinds of leadership development and organizational capacity would serve the ministry across multiple futures, rather than being optimized for just one. And Leverage is the payoff of the entire process: by identifying the driving forces with the highest combined impact and uncertainty, scenario planning directs the ministry's limited resources toward the investments that will produce disproportionate strategic benefit regardless of how those forces resolve.

    The Community Youth Development framework developed in Session 11 is particularly enriched by scenario planning. Villarruel and colleagues (2003) argue that youth-serving organizations cannot address youth outcomes without attending to the organizational and community conditions that enable sustained ministry. Those conditions — funding, demographic context, organizational health, community partnerships — are exactly the categories of driving forces that Fast Forward Planning™ is designed to surface and analyze. A youth ministry leader who has done serious scenario planning work is also a more grounded Community Youth Development practitioner: one who understands the system within which young people are developing, the forces shaping that system, and the organizational investments required to remain a credible, resilient presence within it.

    Think About It

    The Fast Forward Planning™ process begins with a focusing question and ends with strategic priorities. But embedded in every step of the process is an act of formation as much as an act of analysis. To identify driving forces honestly requires humility about what you do not control. To write scenarios in the past tense requires imaginative empathy — the ability to inhabit futures other than the one you prefer. To commit to strategic priorities across multiple scenarios requires the kind of faith that does not depend on certainty. In what ways has this session challenged not just your planning skills but your disposition as a leader toward uncertainty and change?

    Applying Fast Forward Planning™ in Your Ministry Context

    The practical application of Fast Forward Planning™ in a ministry setting follows from the case studies and exercises developed throughout this session, but a few implementation principles are worth naming explicitly.

    Do It With Others

    Scenario planning done in isolation tends to reproduce the planner's existing assumptions. The most powerful scenario planning processes are collaborative: they bring together diverse perspectives on the same system — staff, board members, community members, partner organizations, and the young people themselves. Each perspective will surface driving forces, uncertainties, and plausible future stories that the individual leader would not have identified alone. The Core Value of Interdependence is not merely strategically beneficial in scenario planning — it is methodologically essential.

    Return to the Scenarios Regularly

    Scenarios are not one-time planning artifacts. They are living strategic frameworks that should be revisited as new information becomes available. When an early-warning indicator that the planning team identified begins to materialize — when the funding landscape shifts, when the demographic trend accelerates, when the community relationship deepens in ways that were not anticipated — the scenarios provide an immediate interpretive framework. The ministry that has done scenario planning can say, "This looks like the beginning of Scenario B" and respond with the contingency plans it has already prepared. The ministry that has not done scenario planning simply reacts.

    Connect Scenarios to the Breakthrough Plan

    For DVULI participants, scenario planning is not a standalone exercise — it is a foundational input to the Breakthrough Plan developed across the program's final months. The strategic priorities identified through Fast Forward Planning™ are among the highest-leverage inputs into both the Personal Focus Statement and the Ministry Focus Statement. A Breakthrough Plan that has been informed by rigorous scenario planning is more strategically robust, more honestly calibrated to the uncertainties of the real ministry environment, and more likely to produce the strategic, significant, and sustainable change that DVULI defines as Breakthrough.

    Key Takeaways

    • Goal-based planning alone is insufficient for ministry leadership in contexts characterized by funding volatility, demographic change, and cultural disruption. Scenario planning complements goal-based planning by building adaptive capacity for multiple possible futures.
    • The Fast Forward Planning™ process (Peter O'Donnell) provides a five-step framework accessible to ministry leaders: Focusing Question → Driving Forces → Questions That Matter → Alternative Scenarios → Strategic Priorities.
    • Useful scenarios are plausible, distinctly different, and written from the future looking back — the "remembering the future" technique activates strategic imagination that goal-based planning suppresses.
    • Driving forces should be rated on both Impact and Uncertainty; the highest combined scores identify Critical Uncertainties — the forces most demanding of scenario preparation rather than prediction.
    • The four strategic priority types — Just Do It, Develop Essential Capacities, Prepare Contingency Plans, and Access Additional Capacities — ensure that scenario insight translates into differentiated, executable action.
    • The Big Grid (Monument, Lifeboat, Pioneer, Movement) provides a shared vocabulary for honestly assessing a ministry's current trajectory and committing to the strategic investments required to move toward the Movement quadrant.
    • Luke 14:28–30 frames scenario planning not as a failure of faith but as an act of faithful stewardship — the wisdom invitation to count the cost before building the tower.
    • Scenario planning is most powerful when done collaboratively, revisited regularly, and integrated with the Breakthrough Planning process as a source of high-leverage strategic priorities.

    Looking Ahead

    Session 13 builds directly on the scenario planning framework developed here by turning to the question of resources: once you know what multiple futures might demand of your ministry, how do you identify, cultivate, and access the people, goods, services, and funding relationships needed to remain strategically viable across those futures? The Resource Networking workshop introduces the Discover Total Resources framework, the Golden Circle model for cause-based resource development, and the practical art of telling your ministry's story in ways that inspire partnership. Everything you have developed in this session — your focusing question, your driving forces analysis, your scenarios, your strategic priorities — will become the foundation of a resource development strategy built for resilience rather than dependency.

    References

    Allison, M., & Kaye, J. (2015). Strategic planning for nonprofit organizations: A practical guide and workbook (3rd ed.). Wiley. https://www.wiley.com/en-us/Strategic+Planning+for+Nonprofit+Organizations

    Chermack, T. J. (2011). Scenario planning in organizations: How to create, use, and assess scenarios. Berrett-Koehler. https://www.bkconnection.com/books/title/scenario-planning-in-organizations

    O'Donnell, P. (2016). Fast Forward Planning™ [Training framework]. Used with permission. DVULI Scenario Planning Curriculum.

    Ringland, G., Sparrow, O., & Lustig, P. (2010). Using scenario planning to reshape strategy. MIT Sloan Management Review. https://sloanreview.mit.edu/article/using-scenario-planning-to-reshape-strategy/

    Schoemaker, P. J. H. (1995). Scenario planning: A tool for strategic thinking. MIT Sloan Management Review, 36(2), 25–40. https://sloanreview.mit.edu/article/scenario-planning-a-tool-for-strategic-thinking/

    Schwartz, P. (1991). The art of the long view: Planning for the future in an uncertain world. Currency Doubleday. https://www.penguinrandomhouse.com/books/159616/the-art-of-the-long-view-by-peter-schwartz/

    van der Heijden, K. (1996). Scenarios: The art of strategic conversation (2nd ed.). Wiley. https://www.wiley.com/en-us/Scenarios%3A+The+Art+of+Strategic+Conversation

    Walker, B., & Salt, D. (2006). Resilience thinking: Sustaining ecosystems and people in a changing world. Island Press. https://islandpress.org/books/resilience-thinking

blocks.title: Key Concepts
concepts: Fast Forward Planning™: five-step scenario planning process (Focusing Question → Driving Forces → Questions That Matter → Alternative Scenarios → Strategic Priorities)
Characteristics of useful scenarios: plausible, distinctly different, written from the future looking back
The Big Grid: Monument, Lifeboat, Pioneer, and Movement as four ministry future scenarios
Strategic priorities: Just Do It, Develop Essential Capacities, Prepare Contingency Plans, Access Additional Capacities

items.question: What is the primary reason the chapter argues that traditional goal-based planning alone is insufficient for ministry leaders?
items.correctAnswer: The rate of change in external factors like funding, demographics, and culture makes rigid plans dangerously fragile
items.correctFeedback: The chapter opens by arguing that urban communities, funding landscapes, demographic shifts, and cultural dynamics change so rapidly that a plan built around fixed goals can collapse when any major assumption proves wrong. Scenario planning was developed precisely for conditions of high uncertainty and rapid change.

distractors.text: Goal-based planning requires too much financial investment for most ministries to afford
distractors.feedback: A student might choose this because resource constraints are a real ministry concern. However, the chapter's argument is not about cost but about the fragility of plans that assume a single predictable future in a rapidly changing environment.

distractors.text: Ministry leaders lack the professional training needed to set measurable goals effectively
distractors.feedback: This distractor appeals to the idea that ministry training differs from business training. However, the chapter's point is not that leaders cannot set goals but that even well-set goals become brittle when external conditions shift unpredictably.

distractors.text: Goal-based planning was designed for secular organizations and conflicts with faith-based decision-making
distractors.feedback: A student might assume the chapter frames goal-setting as incompatible with ministry values. In fact, the chapter does not reject goal-based planning outright — it argues that it must be supplemented with scenario planning because of uncertainty, not because of a sacred-secular divide.

items.question: In the Fast Forward Planning™ process, what is the correct sequence of the five steps?
items.correctAnswer: Focusing Question → Driving Forces → Questions That Matter → Alternative Scenarios → Strategic Priorities
items.correctFeedback: The five-step sequence moves logically from defining what you need to know (Focusing Question), to identifying the forces shaping the future (Driving Forces), to surfacing critical uncertainties (Questions That Matter), to constructing plausible future stories (Alternative Scenarios), and finally to committing to high-leverage actions (Strategic Priorities).

distractors.text: Driving Forces → Focusing Question → Alternative Scenarios → Questions That Matter → Strategic Priorities
distractors.feedback: A student might think you need to survey the landscape of forces before asking a question. However, the process intentionally begins with a Focusing Question so that the identification of Driving Forces is targeted and purposeful rather than unfocused.

distractors.text: Focusing Question → Questions That Matter → Driving Forces → Strategic Priorities → Alternative Scenarios
distractors.feedback: This sequence places Questions That Matter before Driving Forces, but in the actual process you must first identify the external and internal forces shaping the future before you can determine which uncertainties among those forces are the critical ones you cannot answer.

distractors.text: Alternative Scenarios → Driving Forces → Focusing Question → Questions That Matter → Strategic Priorities
distractors.feedback: Starting with scenarios might seem intuitive — imagining futures first. However, the process requires that scenarios be built systematically from a focused question, identified forces, and critical uncertainties, not generated from imagination alone.

items.question: Which biblical passage does the chapter use as a philosophical anchor for scenario planning, and how is it interpreted?
items.correctAnswer: Luke 14:28–30 about counting the cost before building a tower, interpreted as a wisdom invitation to plan with both faith and intelligence
items.correctFeedback: The chapter reframes this familiar passage not as a warning against bold action but as an affirmation that wise planning and genuine faith are complementary. Counting the cost is presented as an act of wisdom, not timidity — making it a natural scriptural foundation for scenario planning.

distractors.text: Luke 14:28–30 about counting the cost before building a tower, interpreted as a cautionary tale about the dangers of overcommitting resources
distractors.feedback: This is a common reading of the passage, which is exactly why this distractor is appealing. However, the chapter explicitly rejects this cautionary interpretation and instead frames the passage as an invitation to plan wisely and boldly — combining faith with intelligence.

distractors.text: Proverbs 29:18 about people perishing without vision, interpreted as a call to develop long-range strategic plans
distractors.feedback: This is a well-known planning-related verse that a student might associate with strategic thinking. However, the chapter specifically uses Luke 14:28–30, not Proverbs 29:18, and its emphasis is on planning under uncertainty rather than vision-casting.

distractors.text: James 4:13–15 about not boasting about tomorrow, interpreted as a reminder that all plans must remain flexible before God
distractors.feedback: James 4 does address the uncertainty of the future and might seem like a natural fit for scenario planning. However, the chapter anchors itself in Luke 14:28–30 and frames planning not as presumption to be avoided but as wisdom to be embraced alongside faith.

items.question: What are the two axes that form the Big Grid used to generate the four ministry scenario archetypes?
items.correctAnswer: Stagnation vs. Innovation and Isolation vs. Collaboration
items.correctFeedback: The Big Grid is a two-by-two matrix where one axis measures the ministry's posture toward change (Stagnation vs. Innovation) and the other measures its posture toward relationships with others (Isolation vs. Collaboration). The intersection of these two continuums creates four distinct scenario spaces.

distractors.text: Growth vs. Decline and Internal Focus vs. External Focus
distractors.feedback: These axes sound like standard organizational analysis categories, which makes them tempting. However, the Big Grid specifically frames the dimensions as Stagnation vs. Innovation (about methodology, not size) and Isolation vs. Collaboration (about partnership, not just outward orientation).

distractors.text: Faith vs. Strategy and Individual vs. Community
distractors.feedback: A student might construct these axes from the chapter's themes of faith-based planning and community engagement. However, the Big Grid's actual axes are Stagnation vs. Innovation and Isolation vs. Collaboration — both are strategic postures, and the chapter never sets faith against strategy.

distractors.text: Resources vs. Vision and Tradition vs. Adaptation, which some practitioners argue better captures the tension between financial capacity and aspirational direction
distractors.feedback: These axes capture real tensions in ministry planning, which makes them plausible. However, the Big Grid specifically uses Stagnation vs. Innovation and Isolation vs. Collaboration as its two dimensions, which are related to but distinct from resource-vision and tradition-adaptation frameworks.

items.question: In the Big Grid, which scenario archetype represents a ministry that continues doing the same things without seeking partnerships or collaboration?
items.correctFeedback: Monument sits at the intersection of Stagnation and Isolation — a ministry that neither innovates its methods nor partners with others. It preserves what has always been done, alone, like a monument that stands unchanged regardless of what happens around it.

distractors.text: Lifeboat
distractors.feedback: Lifeboat involves collaboration, which distinguishes it from Monument. A student might confuse the two because both can seem defensive or survival-oriented, but Lifeboat specifically involves drifting together in community — the collaboration axis — while lacking innovation.

distractors.text: Pioneer
distractors.feedback: Pioneer does involve operating alone (Isolation), which it shares with Monument. However, Pioneer is characterized by Innovation rather than Stagnation — the ministry is trying new things, just doing so without collaborative partners.

distractors.text: Movement
distractors.feedback: Movement is actually the opposite of Monument on both axes. It represents Innovation and Collaboration together, making it the Breakthrough scenario. A student might confuse the two if they are not clear on the grid's structure.

items.question: A youth ministry identifies that gentrification is rapidly changing the neighborhood demographics, but the ministry cannot predict whether city policies will accelerate or slow the displacement. In the Fast Forward Planning™ process, how should this uncertainty be classified?
items.correctAnswer: A Question That Matters — a critical uncertainty the ministry cannot answer but must prepare for
items.correctFeedback: Questions That Matter are the critical uncertainties identified in Step 3 of the process. They represent factors the ministry cannot predict or control but that will significantly shape the future. The unpredictable direction of city policy on gentrification is exactly this kind of uncertainty — the ministry must prepare for multiple outcomes.

distractors.text: A Driving Force — an external factor that will shape the ministry's future, such as demographic trends or economic conditions beyond the organization's control
distractors.feedback: Gentrification itself is indeed a Driving Force (Step 2), and a student might classify the entire situation this way. However, the specific uncertainty about whether city policy will accelerate or slow displacement is a Question That Matters — the subset of driving forces whose outcomes are genuinely unpredictable.

distractors.text: A Focusing Question — the central concern about the ministry's future
distractors.feedback: The Focusing Question (Step 1) is the overarching question that frames the entire planning process, such as 'What will our ministry look like in five years?' The gentrification uncertainty is too specific to be the Focusing Question — it is one of several critical uncertainties the process must address.

distractors.text: An Alternative Scenario — a plausible story about one way the future might unfold
distractors.feedback: A student might jump ahead to scenarios because gentrification could be part of a future story. However, Alternative Scenarios (Step 4) are constructed from multiple Questions That Matter combined together. The city policy uncertainty is an input to scenario building, not a scenario itself.

items.question: Which of the four strategic priority categories describes actions a ministry should take immediately because they are valuable regardless of which future scenario unfolds?
items.correctFeedback: Just Do It priorities are actions that make sense across all plausible scenarios. Because they create value no matter what future emerges, there is no reason to delay — the ministry should implement them immediately. This is the most decisive category of strategic response.

distractors.text: Develop Essential Capacities
distractors.feedback: Developing Essential Capacities is about building skills, resources, or infrastructure the ministry will need in multiple scenarios. While also broadly useful, this category emphasizes preparation and capacity-building over immediate action, distinguishing it from the urgency of Just Do It priorities.

distractors.text: Prepare Contingency Plans
distractors.feedback: Contingency Plans are designed for specific scenarios that may or may not occur. A student might confuse them with Just Do It priorities because both are proactive, but contingency plans are conditional — they are activated only if a particular future begins to materialize.

distractors.text: Access Additional Capacities
distractors.feedback: Accessing Additional Capacities involves identifying external resources, partnerships, or skills the ministry does not currently possess but might need. Unlike Just Do It priorities, these are about positioning for potential needs rather than taking universally valuable immediate action.

items.question: Why does the chapter identify the Movement quadrant of the Big Grid as the Breakthrough scenario toward which the entire DVULI curriculum is oriented?
items.correctAnswer: Because it combines Innovation and Collaboration, aligning with all five Core Values, Community Youth Development, and resource networking
items.correctFeedback: Movement represents the convergence of innovating ministry approaches and doing so in collaborative community. The chapter explicitly connects this quadrant to the DVULI curriculum's Core Values, its Community Youth Development framework, and its emphasis on resource networking — all of which require both creative new approaches and deep partnership.

distractors.text: Because it is the only quadrant that guarantees ministry growth and financial sustainability
distractors.feedback: A student might assume the Breakthrough scenario promises guaranteed success. However, scenario planning never guarantees outcomes — Movement is the aspirational direction because it embodies the values and practices the curriculum champions, not because it eliminates risk.

distractors.text: Because it is the most likely scenario to occur based on current trends in urban ministry
distractors.feedback: This reflects a misunderstanding of scenario planning. Scenarios are not predictions ranked by probability — they are plausible alternatives. Movement is the Breakthrough scenario not because it is the most likely but because it represents the ministry posture most consistent with DVULI's values.

distractors.text: Because the other three quadrants all lead to ministry failure within five years
distractors.feedback: A student might assume the other scenarios are simply negative predictions. While Monument, Lifeboat, and Pioneer each have significant limitations, the Big Grid presents them as plausible futures to prepare for, not guaranteed failures. Movement is preferred because of its alignment with core values, not because alternatives always collapse.

items.question: A ministry leader realizes her organization innovates constantly but always works alone, rarely partnering with other churches or community organizations. According to the Big Grid, which scenario archetype best describes her ministry's current posture, and what is its primary risk?
items.correctAnswer: Pioneer — the risk is that innovation without collaboration limits scale, sustainability, and shared learning
items.correctFeedback: Pioneer occupies the Innovation + Isolation quadrant. While innovation is valuable, doing it alone means the ministry bears all the risk, cannot leverage others' strengths, misses shared learning opportunities, and limits the scale of impact. The Big Grid suggests that moving toward the Movement quadrant requires adding collaboration to existing innovation.

distractors.text: Monument — the risk is that the ministry will become irrelevant as the community changes around it
distractors.feedback: Monument is characterized by Stagnation and Isolation. Since this ministry is actively innovating, it does not fit the Monument archetype. A student might choose this if they focus only on the isolation dimension and overlook that the ministry is innovating, not stagnating.

distractors.text: Movement — the risk is that the ministry is moving too fast for its community to keep up
distractors.feedback: Movement requires both Innovation and Collaboration. Since this ministry works alone, it lacks the collaboration dimension essential to Movement. A student might choose this because the ministry is active and creative, but activity alone does not equal Movement.

distractors.text: Lifeboat — the risk is that the ministry is focused on survival rather than thriving
distractors.feedback: Lifeboat combines Stagnation with Collaboration — people drifting together without innovating. This ministry is the opposite: innovating but not collaborating. A student might choose Lifeboat if they associate working alone with a survival mentality, but the grid categorizes it differently.

items.question: A ministry's scenario planning process reveals that training bilingual youth workers is valuable across all four scenarios, while launching a new after-school site only makes sense if a specific community partnership materializes. How should these two actions be categorized as strategic priorities?
items.correctAnswer: Training bilingual youth workers is a Just Do It priority; launching the new site is a Prepare Contingency Plans priority
items.correctFeedback: Actions that create value across all plausible futures are Just Do It priorities — they should be implemented immediately without waiting. Actions that depend on specific conditions materializing are Contingency Plans — they should be designed in advance but activated only when the triggering scenario begins to emerge. This distinction is central to how scenario planning converts insight into action.

distractors.text: Training bilingual youth workers is a Develop Essential Capacities priority; launching the new site is an Access Additional Capacities priority
distractors.feedback: While training does build capacity, the key distinction is that it is valuable in all scenarios, making it a Just Do It priority rather than merely a capacity-building exercise. Similarly, the new site's dependence on a specific partnership makes it a contingency plan, not simply an additional capacity to access.

distractors.text: Both are Develop Essential Capacities priorities because they both strengthen the ministry's readiness for the future
distractors.feedback: A student might group both under capacity development because both prepare the ministry. However, scenario planning insists on distinguishing between actions valuable in all futures (Just Do It) and actions contingent on specific conditions (Contingency Plans). Collapsing this distinction undermines the strategic precision the process provides.

distractors.text: Training bilingual youth workers is a Prepare Contingency Plans priority; launching the new site is a Just Do It priority
distractors.feedback: This reverses the correct categorization. Training that is valuable across all scenarios should not wait for a trigger — it is a Just Do It priority. The new site, which depends on a specific partnership materializing, is conditional and therefore a contingency plan. The categories are defined by whether value depends on a particular future unfolding.

questions.stem: What is the correct first step of the Fast Forward Planning™ process?
options: Ask a Focusing Question about the ministry's critical future concern
Identify the Driving Forces that will shape the ministry's future
Develop Alternative Scenarios describing how the future might unfold
Commit to Strategic Priorities that prepare for multiple futures

feedback.correct: The Fast Forward Planning™ process always begins with a Focusing Question — the critical concern about the ministry's future that gives the entire scenario planning exercise its direction. Without a clear focusing question, the remaining steps lack coherence.
feedback.incorrect: While identifying driving forces, building scenarios, and setting priorities are all part of the process, they come later. The process must begin with a Focusing Question that frames what you are actually planning for — everything else flows from that question.

variants.stem: In the Fast Forward Planning™ framework, which step comes immediately after identifying Driving Forces?
options: Surface Questions That Matter — the critical uncertainties you must prepare for
Commit to Strategic Priorities for the ministry
Write Alternative Scenarios from the future looking back
Return to the Focusing Question and revise it

feedback.correct: After identifying Driving Forces (Step 2), the next step is to surface Questions That Matter (Step 3) — the critical uncertainties whose answers you do not yet know but that will significantly shape the ministry's future. These uncertainties become the raw material for building alternative scenarios.
feedback.incorrect: The five steps follow a deliberate sequence: Focusing Question → Driving Forces → Questions That Matter → Alternative Scenarios → Strategic Priorities. After driving forces, the process moves to critical uncertainties, not directly to scenarios or priorities.

questions.stem: In the Big Grid scenario framework, which quadrant represents the 'Breakthrough' scenario — the one toward which the DVULI curriculum orients all its work?
options: Movement — innovating in collaborative community
Pioneer — innovating alone
Lifeboat — drifting together in community
Monument — doing the same things alone

feedback.correct: The Movement quadrant sits at the intersection of Innovation and Collaboration. It represents the Breakthrough scenario because it combines creative new approaches with partnership and community, aligning with all five DVULI Core Values, Community Youth Development, and resource networking.
feedback.incorrect: Each quadrant occupies a different position on the two axes of Stagnation vs. Innovation and Isolation vs. Collaboration. The Breakthrough scenario requires both innovation (not just doing the same old things) and collaboration (not going it alone), which uniquely defines the Movement quadrant.

variants.stem: A ministry that is stagnating in its approach and operating in isolation from other organizations would fall into which Big Grid quadrant?
options: Monument — maintaining the status quo alone
Lifeboat — surviving through community partnership
Pioneer — boldly forging ahead independently
Movement — driving collaborative change

feedback.correct: The Monument quadrant represents the intersection of Stagnation and Isolation — a ministry that continues doing the same things by itself. The name 'Monument' captures the idea of something that once had meaning but is now a fixed artifact of the past rather than a living, growing organism.
feedback.incorrect: The Big Grid maps ministries on two axes: Stagnation vs. Innovation and Isolation vs. Collaboration. A ministry that is neither innovating nor collaborating sits in the Monument quadrant — not in Pioneer (which involves innovation), Lifeboat (which involves collaboration), or Movement (which involves both).

questions.stem: A youth ministry leader writes a scenario that begins: 'It is 2030. Five years ago, our city experienced a massive wave of gentrification that displaced 60% of the families we served. Here is how we responded...' Which characteristic of useful scenarios does this writing approach best demonstrate?
options: Written from the future looking back
Plausible and evidence-based
Distinctly different from other scenarios
Focused on a single driving force
justifications: Writing from a future vantage point forces the planner to inhabit a possible reality rather than merely speculate about it, making strategic responses feel concrete and testable
Starting with a specific date proves the scenario is based on real demographic data rather than guesswork
Using past tense makes the scenario easier to read than using future tense
Writing from 2030 ensures the scenario covers at least a five-year planning horizon, which is the industry standard

feedback.correct: The phrase 'It is 2030... here is how we responded' places the writer inside the future looking backward. This technique is powerful because it transforms vague speculation into a felt narrative, making strategic responses concrete and psychologically real rather than abstract possibilities.
feedback.incorrect: While plausibility and distinctness are also characteristics of useful scenarios, the specific feature demonstrated here is the retrospective vantage point — the scenario is narrated as if the future has already happened and the writer is recounting what occurred.
feedback.wrongReason: Students sometimes correctly identify the 'future looking back' approach but misunderstand why it matters. It is not about readability or planning horizons — it is about inhabiting a possible future so completely that your strategic thinking becomes concrete rather than speculative.

variants.stem: A ministry team develops two scenarios for their neighborhood's future. Scenario A describes a community where new immigrant families transform the cultural landscape. Scenario B describes a community where aging congregations merge and consolidate. Which characteristic of useful scenarios does this pair best demonstrate?
options: Distinctly different from one another
Written from the future looking back
Plausible and evidence-based
Aligned with the ministry's preferred outcome
justifications: Scenarios must describe genuinely different futures so that strategic planning can account for divergent possibilities rather than variations of the same trajectory
Having two scenarios instead of one ensures that planners avoid confirmation bias by considering exactly two alternatives
Different scenarios prove that the team was creative during the brainstorming process
Distinctly different scenarios are required because each one must match a different quadrant of the Big Grid

feedback.correct: The two scenarios describe fundamentally different demographic futures — one driven by immigration and cultural transformation, the other by aging and institutional consolidation. This distinctness is essential because scenario planning is only valuable when it prepares you for genuinely divergent possibilities, not minor variations of the same trajectory.
feedback.incorrect: While plausibility and the retrospective writing style are also important, the feature highlighted by contrasting two very different demographic futures is distinctness. Scenarios that are too similar fail the core purpose of scenario planning — preparing for fundamentally different possible worlds.
feedback.wrongReason: Students may correctly identify distinctness but justify it by pointing to creativity, the number of scenarios, or Big Grid correspondence. The real reason distinctness matters is strategic: you need to prepare for genuinely divergent futures, not just variations on a single theme.

questions.stem: Evaluate the relationship between these two statements:
questions.assertion: Scenario planning is more appropriate than traditional goal-based planning when a ministry faces high uncertainty about external conditions like funding, demographics, or cultural shifts.
questions.reason: Scenario planning commits the ministry to strategic priorities that prepare for multiple possible futures rather than betting everything on a single predicted outcome.

feedback.correct: Both statements are true, and the reason directly explains the assertion. Scenario planning is better suited to high-uncertainty environments precisely because its output — strategic priorities that work across multiple futures — provides resilience that single-outcome goal-based planning cannot. When external conditions are unpredictable, betting on one predicted future is dangerously fragile.
feedback.incorrect: A common error is treating these as independently true statements. The reason is not just a separate fact — it is the mechanism that explains why scenario planning outperforms goal-based planning under uncertainty. The whole point of exploring alternative scenarios is to generate strategies robust enough to serve across multiple possible futures.

variants.stem: Evaluate the relationship between these two statements:
variants.assertion: The Monument quadrant of the Big Grid represents the most dangerous scenario for a ministry's long-term viability.
variants.reason: The Monument quadrant combines stagnation with isolation, leaving the ministry unable to adapt to change and without partners to provide support or new perspectives.

feedback.correct: Both statements are true, and the reason explains the assertion. Monument ministries face a double vulnerability: they neither innovate (stagnation) nor build relationships with others (isolation). This combination makes them the most brittle — unable to see change coming and lacking the relational infrastructure to respond when it arrives.
feedback.incorrect: Students sometimes argue that Monument is not the 'most dangerous' because Lifeboat or Pioneer also have weaknesses. However, Monument uniquely combines both negative poles — stagnation AND isolation — while Lifeboat at least has collaboration and Pioneer at least has innovation. The dual deficit is precisely what the reason explains.

questions.stem: A ministry team has completed its scenario planning process and identified the following action: 'Regardless of which future unfolds, we must develop a bilingual communication strategy for our programs.' Which strategic priority category does this action best represent?
options: Just Do It — actions to take regardless of which scenario unfolds
Prepare Contingency Plans — actions prepared for a specific scenario
Access Additional Capacities — resources to seek if a particular future emerges
Develop Essential Capacities — core capabilities needed across scenarios

feedback.correct: The phrase 'regardless of which future unfolds' is the defining marker of a 'Just Do It' priority. These are high-leverage actions that make strategic sense no matter which scenario materializes, making them the most urgent items on the ministry's action plan because there is no reason to wait.
feedback.incorrect: The key distinction is in the words 'regardless of which future.' Contingency Plans are prepared for specific scenarios, Additional Capacities are accessed when particular futures emerge, and Essential Capacities are built over time. 'Just Do It' items require no trigger — they are beneficial across all futures and should begin immediately.

variants.stem: During scenario planning, a ministry leader says: 'If gentrification displaces our families, we need a memorandum of understanding with three suburban churches who could host satellite programs.' Which strategic priority category does this represent?
options: Prepare Contingency Plans — actions prepared for a specific possible future
Just Do It — actions to take regardless of which scenario unfolds
Develop Essential Capacities — core capabilities to build now
Access Additional Capacities — resources to seek from outside the ministry

feedback.correct: The conditional trigger 'if gentrification displaces our families' marks this as a Contingency Plan — a prepared response for a specific scenario. The ministry is not acting on it now but is building the plan so they can act quickly if that particular future begins to materialize.
feedback.incorrect: The conditional 'if' is the critical clue. 'Just Do It' items happen regardless of which future unfolds. This action is explicitly tied to a specific scenario (gentrification displacement), making it a contingency plan — a prepared-but-not-yet-activated strategy for one particular future.

questions.stem: In the Developmental Pathway Model from Session 11, what is the correct sequence through which youth progress?
options: Recipients → Resources → Assets → Leaders
Resources → Recipients → Assets → Leaders
Recipients → Assets → Resources → Leaders
Assets → Resources → Recipients → Leaders

feedback.correct: The Developmental Pathway Model moves youth from being Recipients of services, to being recognized as Resources who contribute, to being valued as Assets in the community, to ultimately serving as Leaders. Each stage represents a deeper level of agency and ownership in the community.
feedback.incorrect: The pathway is carefully sequenced to reflect increasing youth agency. It begins with Recipients (youth receiving services), not Resources or Assets. The progression from Recipients → Resources → Assets → Leaders mirrors the Community Youth Development philosophy that every young person can grow from receiving care to leading community transformation.

variants.stem: Community Youth Development (CYD) rests on a dual conviction. Which statement best captures both halves of the CYD principle from Session 11?
options: Every community develops its youth, and every young person can contribute to community development
Every church must serve youth, and every youth must eventually serve in a church
Every youth program needs funding, and every funder needs measurable youth outcomes
Every young person has potential, and every community has the resources to unlock it

feedback.correct: The CYD principle is explicitly dual-directional: communities shape youth (for better or worse), AND youth can shape their communities. This mutuality distinguishes CYD from one-directional betterment models that only see youth as recipients of adult services.
feedback.incorrect: While several options sound positive and related to youth ministry, the specific CYD formulation emphasizes the bidirectional relationship between community and youth. It is not about churches, funding, or abstract potential — it is about the mutual developmental relationship between communities and young people.

questions.stem: Classify each statement about scenario planning and the Big Grid framework:

statements.text: A useful scenario must predict what will most likely happen in the future
The Movement quadrant involves both innovation and collaboration
Driving forces identified in Step 2 include both external factors (demographics, funding) and internal factors
A ministry can occupy more than one Big Grid quadrant simultaneously across different program areas
Strategic priorities from scenario planning should focus exclusively on the most desirable scenario
The Pioneer quadrant leads to sustainable ministry transformation
Questions That Matter have clear, known answers at the time they are identified

feedback.correct: The key distinctions hinge on understanding that scenarios explore plausibility rather than prediction, that strategic priorities must serve multiple futures, and that Questions That Matter are defined precisely by the fact that their answers are unknown. The Big Grid quadrants describe tendencies, and a ministry can exhibit different quadrant characteristics in different program areas.
feedback.incorrect: The most common confusion involves treating scenarios as predictions (they explore plausible alternatives, not forecasts), believing strategic priorities should favor the preferred scenario (they must prepare for multiple futures), and misunderstanding Questions That Matter as having known answers (their power comes from embracing uncertainty). Pioneer ministries innovate but lack collaborative support, making sustainability possible but not guaranteed.

variants.stem: Classify each statement about the scenario planning process and strategic priorities:

statements.text: Scenarios should be written from the future looking back
'Just Do It' priorities require waiting until a specific scenario begins to materialize
A Focusing Question can be revised after examining driving forces and uncertainties
The Monument quadrant can sustain effective ministry in a stable environment
Contingency Plans are activated regardless of which future unfolds
Effective scenario planning requires exploring scenarios that the ministry hopes will not happen

feedback.correct: Key boundary cases: Monument ministries can persist in truly stable environments, but stability is increasingly rare. Contingency Plans are by definition scenario-specific (unlike 'Just Do It' priorities). A Focusing Question may sometimes be refined as the process reveals new insights. Effective scenario planning always includes unfavorable scenarios because preparation requires confronting uncomfortable possibilities.
feedback.incorrect: Students commonly confuse 'Just Do It' priorities (act immediately, regardless of scenario) with Contingency Plans (prepare but activate only if a specific future emerges). They also mistakenly assume Monument is always unsustainable — in a truly stable, unchanging context it can persist — and that scenarios should only explore hoped-for futures.

questions.stem: The biblical anchor for this session's scenario planning philosophy is Jesus' teaching about counting the cost before building a tower, found in Luke chapter 14. At what verse number does this specific passage begin?

feedback.correct: The passage is Luke 14:28–30. Jesus asks, 'Suppose one of you wants to build a tower. Won't you first sit down and estimate the cost to see whether you have enough money to complete it?' This is not a call to timidity but a wisdom invitation to plan with both faith and intelligence — the very heart of scenario planning.
